children.name: Desmodus
children.description: Vampire bats — the only mammals that feed exclusively on blood (hematophagy). The common vampire bat feeds primarily on large mammals, landing near sleeping cattle, horses, or other animals and using heat-sensing pits to locate blood vessels close to the surface, then making a small incision with razor-sharp incisors and lapping the flowing blood. Anticoagulants in the saliva (called draculin) keep the blood flowing. A vampire bat that fails to feed on any given night will die within 60 hours; to prevent this, well-fed bats in a roost regularly regurgitate blood to feed hungry roost-mates — one of the most sophisticated examples of reciprocal altruism in any non-human animal.

children.name: Desmodus rotundus
children.description: Feeds almost exclusively on cattle blood; can walk and leap on the ground with surprising agility to approach prey. The draculin anticoagulant in its saliva is under investigation as a potential stroke treatment.

children.name: Glossophaga
children.description: Nectar bats — hovering specialists with elongated snouts and brush-tipped tongues longer than their skulls that allow them to access the nectar of deep tubular flowers. They are the primary pollinators of many cactus and agave species, including the blue agave used to make tequila. Losing nectar bat populations can collapse entire plant communities — some desert plants depend entirely on these bats for pollination. Nectar bats have the fastest tongues relative to body size of any mammal, lapping nectar at up to 15 times per second.

children.name: Glossophaga soricina
children.description: The most widespread nectar bat in Latin America. Critical pollinator of more than 150 plant species including agaves, cacti, and bananas. Can hover like a hummingbird for up to 1.5 seconds while feeding.

children.name: Artibeus
children.description: Jamaican fruit bats and their relatives — robust, short-faced bats built for processing hard tropical fruits. Artibeus jamaicensis is one of the most abundant Neotropical bats and a crucial seed disperser for figs, wild avocados, and hundreds of other forest trees. They carry fruit to eating roosts away from the parent tree, dropping seeds in forest gaps where they have the best chance of germination. Artibeus bats can carry fruit up to 50% of their own body weight and process a mango-sized fruit in under a minute.

children.name: Artibeus jamaicensis
children.description: Among the most abundant mammals in lowland Neotropical forest. Disperses seeds of figs and forest trees up to 2 km from the parent tree. Has a distinctive white facial stripe.

children.name: Phyllostomus
children.description: Spear-nosed bats — large, omnivorous phyllostomids with unusually broad dietary ranges. Unlike most bats that specialise in one food type, greater spear-nosed bats eat insects, frogs, lizards, mice, other bats, nectar, pollen, and fruit. The erect, spear-like noseleaf gives the family its name. Phyllostomus bats are among the largest Neotropical bats and roost in caves in groups of hundreds to thousands, where they maintain large stable social groups with complex vocalisations that function as group signatures.

children.name: Phyllostomus hastatus
children.description: One of the largest bats in the Americas with a wingspan approaching 50 cm. Harem groups of up to 25 females share a territory. Females produce group-specific 'screech calls' to identify roost-mates in the dark.

children.name: Carollia
children.description: Short-tailed fruit bats — small to medium phyllostomids that are among the most abundant mammals in Neotropical lowland forest. They specialise in the fruit of pioneer plants — particularly Piper (pepper plants) — and are therefore among the first seed dispersers to recolonise forest gaps and disturbed areas. In heavily deforested landscapes, Carollia bats may be the only remaining seed dispersers capable of beginning forest regeneration. Their ecological importance in disturbed habitats makes them a flagship species for the conservation value of bats.

children.name: Carollia perspicillata
children.description: Often the single most abundant bat species at Neotropical lowland sites. Specialises in Piper fruits. Has become a model species in bat research due to its tolerance of captivity and active breeding in labs.
